subject: Column space and nullspace
concepts: Vector Space: A collection of vectors where any two vectors can be added, and any vector can be multiplied by a scalar, with the result always staying in the collection. Simply put, a vector space is a set of vectors that is 'closed' under addition and scalar multiplication — you can never escape the space by combining its members.

Subspace: A subset of vectors within a larger vector space that itself satisfies the vector space requirements (closed under addition and scalar multiplication), and must always contain the zero vector. Think of it as a smaller 'club' of vectors living inside a bigger space, where combining any two club members always produces another club member.

Union vs. Intersection of Subspaces: The union of two subspaces (all vectors in either one) is generally NOT a subspace, because adding a vector from one subspace to a vector from another can land outside both. The intersection (all vectors in both) IS always a subspace, because any two vectors satisfying both sets of requirements will still satisfy both after addition or scaling.

Column Space C(A): The set of all linear combinations of the columns of a matrix A. For an m×n matrix, the column space is a subspace of R^m. The column space answers the question: for which right-hand side vectors b does Ax = b have a solution? The answer is: exactly when b is in the column space of A.

Ax = b is Solvable if and only if b is in the Column Space: The system Ax = b has a solution if and only if b can be written as a linear combination of the columns of A (i.e., b is in C(A)). In plain terms, b must be reachable by some combination of A's columns — if it isn't, no solution exists.

Null Space N(A): The set of all vectors x that satisfy Ax = 0. For an m×n matrix, the null space is a subspace of R^n (since x has n components). It always contains the zero vector, and is a genuine subspace because the sum of two solutions to Ax = 0 is also a solution, and any scalar multiple of a solution is also a solution.

Proof that the Null Space is a Subspace: If Av = 0 and Aw = 0, then A(v + w) = Av + Aw = 0 + 0 = 0, so v + w is also in the null space. Similarly, A(12v) = 12Av = 0. This relies on the distributive law of matrix multiplication. In simple terms, solutions to Ax = 0 'play nicely' with addition and scaling, which is exactly what makes them a subspace.

Solutions to Ax = b (b ≠ 0) Do NOT Form a Subspace: When the right-hand side b is not the zero vector, the solution set cannot be a subspace because the zero vector is not a solution. The solution set looks like a shifted subspace (a line or plane not passing through the origin). A key insight: subspaces must always pass through the origin.

Two Natural Ways to Describe a Subspace: (1) Span — give a set of vectors and take all their linear combinations (as with the column space). (2) Equations — give a set of conditions the vectors must satisfy (as with the null space, defined by Ax = 0). Both approaches always produce valid subspaces.
examples: Showing that a plane through the origin in R^3 is a subspace: any two vectors in the plane sum to a vector still in the plane, and scaling a vector in the plane keeps it in the plane.

Showing that the union of a plane P and a line L in R^3 is NOT a subspace: taking one vector from P and one from L and adding them typically produces a vector outside both P and L, violating the closure requirement.
Showing that the intersection of a plane P and a line L in R^3 IS a subspace: if L is not contained in P, their intersection is just the zero vector {0}, which is a valid (trivial) subspace.

Proving in general that the intersection of any two subspaces S and T is a subspace: if v and w are both in S and both in T, then v + w is in S (since S is a subspace) and in T (since T is a subspace), so v + w is in S ∩ T; the same argument holds for scalar multiples.
Identifying the column space of the 4×3 matrix A = [[1,1,2],[2,1,3],[3,1,4],[4,1,5]] as a subspace of R^4, and observing that it cannot span all of R^4 since only 3 columns are present.

Solving Ax = b for A = [[1,1,2],[2,1,3],[3,1,4],[4,1,5]] and b = [1,2,3,4] (the first column of A): the solution is x = [1,0,0], illustrating that b is in the column space when it is one of A's own columns.
Solving Ax = b for the same A with b = [1,1,1,1] (the second column of A): the solution is x = [0,1,0].

Identifying that column 3 = column 1 + column 2 in A = [[1,1,2],[2,1,3],[3,1,4],[4,1,5]], so the third column is redundant and the column space is a 2-dimensional subspace of R^4 (the third column can be discarded without changing the column space).
Finding the null space of A = [[1,1,2],[2,1,3],[3,1,4],[4,1,5]] by solving Ax = 0: the solution set is all vectors of the form c·[1,1,−1] for any scalar c, which is a line through the origin in R^3.

Demonstrating that the solutions to Ax = b with b = [1,2,3,4] and A as above do NOT form a subspace: one solution is [1,0,0] and another is [0,−1,1]; the zero vector [0,0,0] is not a solution, so the solution set fails to be a subspace.

Proving the null space is a subspace using the distributive law: if Av = 0 and Aw = 0, then A(v+w) = Av + Aw = 0 + 0 = 0, so v + w is in the null space; and A(12v) = 12Av = 12·0 = 0, so 12v is also in the null space.
analogy: The two natural ways to describe a subspace are compared to two different ways of defining a group: either by listing its members and saying 'take all combinations' (span/column space approach), or by stating rules that members must obey (equations approach/null space).
reviewed_transcript: What if I told you a matrix is really just a map to hidden treasure? Every matrix A has a column space — the set of all targets you can actually reach by taking linear combinations of its columns. Think of the columns as directions you're allowed to move in. The column space is every point you can get to in this way. A plane through the origin in three dimensions? That's a column space. A line through the origin? Also a column space. Both are subspaces — special sets where adding or scaling vectors never kicks you out of the set. Then there's the null space: all the inputs x where Ax lands exactly on zero. These aren't dead ends — they reveal the hidden structure of the system. Master column spaces and null spaces, and you unlock the ability to solve equations, understand what's possible, and see exactly where a system's power — and its limits — truly lie.
